captions.text: Don't overlook the early signs of anime girl  infestation. If you're finding mysterious hair
ornaments scattered around the room, that all  your lace doilies have gone missing, or that a
hole has appeared in the skirting board designed  to accommodate a completely ridiculous hairstyle,
maybe it's time to start putting a few traps  down baited with vanilla pudding and shy, poorly
characterized young men. 'Cause you know, for  every anime girl you see, there's probably 10 more
in the walls. Breeding and doing ahegao faces. If  you don't put the kibosh on this early, you end
up like Code Vein, a gritty, post-apocalyptic  Soulslike but where all the male characters
are proportioned like Give Way signs and all the  female characters give flashes of tit and panty
shot like a political campaign gives out bumper  stickers. But I guess you can't deny it attracts
certain audiences because now we've got a sequel,  Code Vein 2, somehow even more post-apocalyptic
than the first one and with the anime infection  showing no signs of slowing. It's also graduated
from anime Dark Souls to anime Elden Ring. It kind  of plays like Elden Ring after you add a bunch of
visual mods designed by people who have to clean  themselves in the shower with a hammer and chisel.
Code Vein 2 is an unusual case of a post-POST  apocalyptic setting in that it's set in a world
that's absolutely hyperfucked, and our job is  to travel back in time to when it was merely
turbofucked and make connections with five  legendary heroes who are destined to help
delay the second, for realsies apocalypse by, in  technical terms, totally Lord Gwynning themselves.
Then we can return to the present and kick the  shit out of the boss monsters they've turned
into now they've been holding back the end of all  things for a hundred years. And killing them will
somehow stop the apocalypse they were trying to  stop? I dunno. It's confusing, partly because time
travel always is, partly because it was explained  to me by a giant anime lady with tits like two
morbidly obese people fucking in a child-sized  sleeping bag so every time she explained it,
all I could think about was whatever the  fuck her bra was made from would probably
revolutionize contemporary science. Before  all that, though, I had to make my character,
and in the spirit of "when in Rome," I made  myself a lovely anime girl just so I'd have
a nice bum to look at for the whole game. An  attitude I think the game might have anticipated.
Because even with the hips slider set to  lowest, I was sporting a cake that could
gouge your eyes out and a thigh gap that would  be the envy of an electricity pylon. Anyway,
the first thing we see on game start  is a pale, waifish anime girl with sad,
trusting eyes and an exposed heart letting us  know that she'll be our ride or die waifu going
forward. "So much for the maidenless run," I  thought to myself before I glanced down below
her waist. "Jesus Christ. Now I know why your  upper body looks so undernourished. Talk about
the Great British Cake-off." The girl's name  is Lou, and incidentally, this game is full of
extremely anime people with very mundane names.  There's dudes built like old-fashioned telegraph
machines with cyan hair and gold filigree all over  their face, and it never failed to make me laugh
when they introduced themselves as Craig or Steve.  Anyway, Lou is the one with time travel powers,
and in order to activate them, she very sadly  holds her arms out and we have to give her
a great big lovely cuddle. And already I've lost  count of how many times this game has made me say,
"Oh, come the fuck on." Next you'll be  telling me one of her nipples doubles
as the master volume control. But I can't  blame anime for all the game's weird quirks.
For example, there's a weird trend for characters  wearing their jackets half off their shoulders.
Perhaps the designer thought this would be  their "thing," like Tetsuya Nomura's belts
and mismatched trouser legs, and I guess it  looks cool; after all, there's nothing cooler
than getting your hood caught on every doorknob  you walk past. For all of this, I was enjoying
the game for a while. It's a sort of snackier  version of Elden Ring with a much smaller map,
and you get the post-apocalyptic anime equivalent  of a demon horse to ride around it on—that is to
say, a ridiculously proportioned yet curiously  fragile motorbike that your current, ridiculously
proportioned NPC partner rides pillion like a  poorly folded deckchair nailed to a truck bed. And
it can be fun to take time to explore the little  nooks and crannies of the world to find upgrade
items. We can time travel to different eras, but  the world basically looks the same in each one,
so it can sometimes be a smidge confusing when  we're trying to figure out when a specific side
quest objective actually exists. At the outset,  you're given three regions to explore with a
legendary hero representing each, and in theory,  you can pursue them in whatever order you choose.
But there's a definite order of increasing  difficulty, so you'll probably start with the
scrappy anime girl who only wears dirty underpants  and a sports bra and presumably missed the "dress
up" part of the "dress up sexy" remit and end  with the posh anime girl whose tits are constantly
threatening to escape from her corset with the  force of two 10-megaton hydrogen bombs. Combat is
functional, if a little bit flabby; it's all about  light attacks, heavy attacks, and super attacks
powered by MP gathered by doing the other kind  of super attacks, and much like with Elden Ring,
if you stick with it long enough, you'll almost  inevitably find a way to break it. For me,
that was when I realized we were doing the Dark  Souls thing where you can still backstab enemies
even if they're aware, and about 90 percent of  the minibosses are just elevated regular enemies,
so I'd just go for chain backstabs and keep  a book on hand to get me through the overlong
takedown animations. The only sticking point  then was the big boss fights, such as when posh
anime girl turns into a big-titted skeleton—and  no, I don't know how a skeleton gets big tits;
maybe she's keeping her pet chinchillas warm—but  you've got a permanent NPC partner who distracts
the boss and donates their health when you're  low, so things never get piss-takingly difficult.
The problem with the anime art style at this point  is that it's impossible to predict how devastating
an attack will be just from paying attention.  Like, a dude will spin around and a fucking neon
spectral scythe blade will fly across the arena  because he did a single armpit fart. But I'll tell
you the precise moment I got bored of Code Vein  2 and quit. In the second area, you're palling
around with this mercenary dude, invading an enemy  tech base, and said tech base consists of several
identical bland metallic rooms unintuitively  connected and full of identical baddies where
it's very easy to get confused and turned around,  and it was generally about as fun as emptying the
dishwasher, but I got through it—boss fight,  angsty cutscenes, anime anime blah blah blah.
And then I moved on to the third area, where  towards the end, I was in the midst of a thrilling
chase sequence through a hospital when the ground  suddenly, literally collapsed beneath my feet, and
when the dust cleared, I was in another fucking  tech base identical to the last. As if to say,
"Hey, sorry to interrupt, but I heard you liked  being really bored! Surprise!" In that moment,
I lost my inclination to continue. So, make of  that what you will. If you bounced off Elden Ring
because you found it too big and intimidating with  not enough tits or filigree, then maybe you won't
bounce off Code Vein 2 unless you go into the  anime cuddles with too much of a running start.
